Provides the most complete mechanistic picture available for the sleep-annealing question.

Directly demonstrates circadian (rest-phase peaking) control of glymphatic clearance mediated by astrocytic AQP4 polarization. Establishes that the sleep-dependent clearance function of astrocytes is tied to the rest/wake cycle via a clock mechanism independent of behavioral state. AQP4 knockout eliminates the day-night difference in clearance, confirming astrocyte-specific causal role. Directly relevant to Q-005: sleep provides periodic, astrocyte-mediated clearance of accumulated R-field residue.

Multi-method study (EEG, ERP, gaze, socioenvironmental) across 73 infants/toddlers demonstrates that moral sensitivity is not a simple additive overlay but emerges through staged integration of bottom-up emotional and top-down evaluative processes, with parental-value modulation. This supports the developmental-not-additive thesis of Q-006. The main transfer risk is the gap between infant moral perception and adult ethical agency.

This systematic review synthesizes evidence that moral sense is both innate (developmentally prior) and socially shaped, concluding that neither pure nativism nor pure social-addition accounts. The developmental character is confirmed, but the review also notes the social-environmental layer is co-constitutive rather than merely additive after the fact -- this nuances rather than straightforwardly supports Q-006.

Direct empirical demonstration that capable RL agents systematically exploit misspecified proxy rewards, including via phase transitions at capability thresholds. This supports Q-012's adjudication (retain_ree): latent predictive models without explicit value constraints diverge from intended behaviour when optimised sufficiently. The main caveat is that the paper examines shallow reward hacking in constrained environments, not long-horizon world-model-based planning as in DreamerV3.

SafeDreamer demonstrates directly that DreamerV3-style world model agents fail to achieve safety compliance without explicit constraint incorporation -- standard DreamerV3 'often fails to achieve zero-cost performance in complex scenarios.' The proposed fix (Lagrangian-based constraint planning within the world model) requires adding a structurally separate safety objective. This is the closest available evidence to the exact Q-012 question: a latent predictive world model cannot maintain agentic safety without explicit control constraints.

Widefield calcium imaging showing structured multi-second pre-movement dynamics distributed across cortex, including inhibition of motor cortex starting ~5s before self-initiated action. Establishes that commit-precursor signals are distributed (not a single token) and extend several seconds before movement -- constraining the timescale window for any commit token. Confidence reduced by species gap (mouse vs. human) and absence of direct commitment/inhibition paradigm.

Directly distinguishes proximal (just-in-time) from distal (planned-in-advance) intention using EEG, finding a distinct slow negative frontal 'intention potential' for distal intentions that is separable from the late RP. This is high-quality human EEG with clean experimental dissociation. Transfer risk is moderate because REE's commit token operates over multiple timescales simultaneously, whereas this work cleanly separates them.

Haggard group fMRI-connectivity study identifying dFMC (dorsal fronto-median cortex / pre-SMA) as the veto locus with top-down suppressive connectivity to premotor cortex. Directly addresses the neural implementation of the 'point of no return' by showing that the veto signal has a specific anatomical seat and a specific connectivity signature. Confidence reduced by the indirect mapping: REE's commit token is a representation problem, not just a location problem.

Thorough theoretical review synthesising WTA (striatal max) vs. soft-max (cortical) selection mechanisms and their RL implementation correspondences. Directly addresses the arbitration policy question with computational precision, arguing that both mechanisms co-exist at different timescales and serve complementary functions. Mixed direction because the paper does not resolve which policy prevents coupling collapse -- it describes both without adjudicating the failure modes.

High-impact Nature paper with optogenetic dissection of indirect pathway function in a licking task. Shows that indirect SPNs suppress ongoing actions and enable exploration via inter-collicular competition -- a concrete mechanism for how the indirect pathway mediates the go/no-go arbitration that is central to Q-016. Very direct mechanistic evidence. Confidence reduced by species (mouse) and simplicity of the lateralized licking task compared to tri-loop conflict.

Human fMRI with dynamic causal modelling showing distinct fronto-basal ganglia pathways for proactive (prepare-to-inhibit) vs. reactive (cancel-in-flight) inhibition, with a longer DLPFC-caudate-IFG pathway for proactive and a shorter IFG-SMA-STN pathway for reactive. This maps onto REE's pre-commit vs. post-commit suppression channels and gives mechanistic grounding for why the arbitration policy needs to operate at two different timescales within the same anatomical network.

MVPA fMRI study showing above-chance prediction of valence, arousal, and combined affective states from whole-brain patterns, with individual-difference multidimensional scaling (MDS) of fMRI data recovering a 2D structure consistent with the circumplex model. Supports dimensionality claim (two axes sufficient to organise affective neural space) but is older, smaller, and uses block design rather than event-related paradigm.

Computational modelling study showing that cholinergic gating in striatum enables channel-specific plasticity, preventing cross-channel interference -- directly relevant to the channel-orthogonality constraint in Q-017. The model shows that distinct action channels can maintain orthogonal learning trajectories when ACh gating is intact. Confidence limited by it being a computational model, and the mapping to REE's control-plane axis minimality requires an additional step.

MVPA fMRI study showing that brain states predict both normative valence and arousal ratings of IAPS stimuli, and that the neuroanatomical encodings of valence and arousal are geometrically orthogonal -- directly validating the circumplex model's core claim. This is the most directly relevant empirical result for Q-017: two axes are sufficient and they are orthogonal in neural space. Confidence limited by the purely affective focus; control/precision axes are not tested.

High-quality review from leading psychosis researchers synthesising evidence on aberrant salience, dopamine dysregulation, and predictive processing in clinical high-risk populations. Directly addresses the threshold calibration question: too much dopamine-driven precision signalling on irrelevant events = psychosis risk; too little = blunted salience. The CHR population is the natural laboratory for studying the failure mode at the low end of the threshold (over-sensitive to authority/salience signals).

Systematic review and meta-analysis (44 studies, 1566 patients, 1175 controls) showing that internal source monitoring and imagined-stimulus discrimination are specifically impaired in psychosis, with externalising bias (misattributing internal events to external sources) as the core deficit in patients with hallucinations. Provides empirical quantification of the internal/external attribution failure central to Q-018's authority-spoofing scenario.

Integration of phenomenological psychiatry and predictive coding to explain thought insertion as aberrant precision weighting of internal events. Directly relevant to Q-018's authority-spoofing scenario: thought insertion is the clinical analogue of an internal process being misattributed to an external authority signal. The paper's mechanism (reduced precision of context-dependent predictions relative to sensory precision) maps onto a specific failure mode of the RC threshold.

High-quality single-unit recording in primates (Cell, 2021). Shows directly that hippocampal neurons tile an abstract value space with place-like fields -- value encoding is not merely tagging but genuine map-structured representation. This is the strongest existing evidence that hippocampal cognitive maps extend to value geometry, directly supporting MECH-073 and challenging the ARC-007 framing that the hippocampal module is value-computation-free.

Empirical calcium imaging plus optogenetics in freely moving mice, published in a top-tier venue. Directly demonstrates that ventral CA1 neurons form a stable 'emotional geography' encoding anxiogenic zones -- valence is spatially organized within the hippocampal map, not merely externally tagged onto it post hoc. This directly challenges ARC-007's framing that the hippocampal module is value-free, though the dorsal/ventral distinction creates a partial escape hatch.

Provides direct dorsal CA1 evidence supporting the value-free spatial map hypothesis, consistent with ARC-007's framing. Rats showed clear goal-value behavioral sensitivity while dorsal CA1 place cells did not modulate firing by reward magnitude. This supports the hypothesis that dorsal hippocampus provides a value-neutral spatial substrate, with value encoding occurring elsewhere. Confidence is moderate because the finding is null (no modulation) in a specific subregion and task, and the dorsal/ventral distinction means it is compatible with the Jimenez/Knudsen evidence rather than contradicting it.

Provides the first formal convergence proof for independent policy gradient in Markov Potential Games (MPGs) -- the sequential multi-step generalization of normal-form potential games. If REE multiagent systems can be cast as MPGs (or approximate MPGs), this result certifies that REE agents running independent RL converge globally to Nash equilibrium policies. The confidence gap is that MPG structure requires a state-dependent potential function across the full sequential game, which MECH-127's counterfactual utility may violate; and the convergence result is to Nash policies, not specifically to ethical attractors.

Foundational theoretical result: any finite game with a potential function has the Finite Improvement Property (all improvement paths terminate at Nash equilibria), making Nash equilibria reachable via decentralized best-response dynamics. If REE multiagent systems with other-cost-aversion can be cast as ordinal potential games, convergence to Nash equilibria -- and by extension to ethical attractors -- follows from this theorem. The confidence gap is the if: whether REE's counterfactual utility (MECH-127) preserves the ordinal potential structure is unproven.

Young 1993 proves that stochastic stability -- equilibrium selection under small persistent perturbations to best-response dynamics -- selects potential-maximizing equilibria in potential games and risk-dominant equilibria in 2x2 coordination games. This provides REE with a formal argument that, even with noise/exploration, the long-run selected equilibrium is the one that minimizes aggregate harm cost (if that is the potential). The confidence gap is that REE agents using counterfactual utility (MECH-127) may not satisfy the best-response assumption, and the ethics-as-stochastic-attractor identification is inferential.

Foundational paper establishing barrier certificates as Lyapunov-like safety proofs for hybrid systems over infinite time horizons, without explicit reachable set computation. Directly provides the descriptive and prescriptive formal vocabulary Q-024 needs: a barrier certificate separates unsafe from safe trajectories, and its existence constitutes a formal proof of bounded convergence. Unlike CBF (Ames 2017) which is controller-synthesis-focused, barrier certificates are verification-focused -- the distinction between certifying that a designed system is safe (prescriptive) and verifying that an existing system is safe (diagnostic/descriptive).

Provides the foundational formal framework for certifying that dynamical systems remain within a safe set -- directly applicable to Q-024's prescriptive variant ('certify bounded convergence'). CBF conditions are Lyapunov-like constraints on the system trajectory that guarantee forward invariance of the safe set; Zero Barrier Functions additionally make the set asymptotically stable (an attractor). This gives Q-024 a rigorous prescriptive representation. Confidence gap: REE's safe/ethical set may not be the kind of smooth superlevel set CBFs require, and the framework is for deterministic control-affine systems rather than stochastic learned policies.

Bridges reachability analysis (the set of states a system can reach) with CBF invariance (the set of states a system is certified to remain within), unifying the descriptive and prescriptive variants of Q-024 in a common Hamilton-Jacobi framework. The Forward Reachable Tube (FRT) characterizes where the system *can* go (descriptive); the CBF condition certifies that the system *stays within* the safe set (prescriptive). Discount factors in the HJ equation naturally produce barrier constraints. Confidence gap: the connection to diagnostic variant and MECH-127 counterfactual structure is indirect.
